app.title: History of the Transistor
app.subtitle: Interactive visualizations from vacuum tubes to modern CMOS
era1.title: The Vacuum Tube (Triode)
era1.subtitle: The first electronic amplifier
era1.narrative.p1: Before transistors, vacuum tubes were the only way to amplify electrical signals. Lee De Forest's Audion tube (1906) added a wire grid between the cathode and anode of a diode. By varying the voltage on this grid, a small signal could control a much larger current — the essence of amplification.
era1.narrative.p2: ENIAC (1945), one of the first general-purpose computers, used 17,468 vacuum tubes. They consumed 150 kilowatts of power, filled a room, and tubes burned out every few days. The need for something smaller, cooler, and more reliable drove the invention of the transistor.
era1.narrative.p3: The grid voltage controls electron flow: when positive, electrons pass through the grid to the anode. When negative, the grid repels electrons back toward the cathode, cutting off current flow.
era1.diagram.label: Triode vacuum tube cross-section showing cathode, grid, and anode
era2.title: The Bipolar Junction Transistor
era2.subtitle: The solid-state revolution
era2.narrative.p1: On December 23, 1947, at Bell Labs, John Bardeen and Walter Brattain demonstrated the first working transistor. William Shockley later developed the junction transistor. All three shared the 1956 Nobel Prize in Physics.
era2.narrative.p2: An NPN transistor has three layers of semiconductor: N-type (excess electrons) on the outside, P-type (excess holes) in the middle. The base region is intentionally made very thin. When a small current flows into the base, it allows a much larger current to flow from collector to emitter — this is current amplification.
era2.narrative.p3: The key insight: the thin base region means most electrons from the emitter 'punch through' to the collector without recombining. The ratio of collector current to base current is β (beta), typically 100-300.
era2.diagram.label: NPN bipolar junction transistor cross-section showing emitter, base, and collector regions
era3.title: The MOSFET
era3.subtitle: The field-effect revolution
era3.narrative.p1: Mohamed Atalla and Dawon Kahng at Bell Labs created the metal-oxide-semiconductor field-effect transistor (MOSFET) in 1959. Unlike BJTs, MOSFETs are voltage-controlled — the gate draws essentially zero current.
era3.narrative.p2: The NMOS structure: a P-type silicon substrate with two N-type regions (source and drain). A thin layer of silicon dioxide (SiO₂) insulates the metal gate from the substrate. The doping creates free charge carriers: phosphorus atoms (N-type) donate electrons, boron atoms (P-type) create holes.
era3.narrative.p3: When gate voltage exceeds the threshold voltage (Vth), the electric field attracts electrons to the surface beneath the gate, forming a conducting channel. This 'inversion layer' connects source to drain. No physical contact with the gate — pure electrostatic control. This is why MOSFETs can be made incredibly small.
era3.diagram.label: NMOS transistor cross-section showing P-type substrate, N-type source and drain, gate oxide, and metal gate
era4.title: CMOS — Complementary MOS
era4.subtitle: The technology that powers everything
era4.narrative.p1: Frank Wanlass at Fairchild Semiconductor patented CMOS in 1963. The key idea: pair an NMOS transistor with a PMOS transistor. When one is ON, the other is OFF. This complementary switching means almost zero static power consumption.
era4.narrative.p2: In a CMOS inverter, when the input is HIGH (1), the NMOS pulls the output to ground while the PMOS is OFF. When the input is LOW (0), the PMOS pulls the output to Vdd while the NMOS is OFF. There's never a direct path from Vdd to ground in steady state.
era4.narrative.p3: CMOS enabled Moore's Law — the observation that transistor density doubles roughly every two years. From the first commercial CMOS chips (10μm, ~1970) to today's 3nm process, transistors have shrunk over 3,000×. Every modern processor, phone, and smart device uses CMOS.
era4.vtc.title: Voltage Transfer Characteristic
era4.scaling.title: Technology Scaling: Moore's Law
era4.diagram.label: CMOS inverter showing complementary NMOS and PMOS transistor pair
